project_description.text: The Bureau of Land Management's Cedar City District proposes a livestock grazing management program on about 2,567,466 acres of public land across roughly 210 allotments in Kane, Garfield, and Washington Counties, Utah, and Coconino County, Arizona, known as the Kanab/Escalante (K/E) area. The preferred Alternative 5 would apply specific grazing systems on 129 allotments, continuous seasonal management on 60, and eliminate grazing on 21, starting at 68,298 AUMs for livestock and growing to 91,444 AUMs over 24 years. Implementation was scheduled for the March 1981 grazing fee year.

affected_community.text: The plan area is largely rural, with ranching families in Kane and Garfield Counties who have grazed cattle since the 1870s and whose lifestyles and identities are tied to livestock. About 282 ranch operations (small, medium, and large scale) hold BLM permits, with BLM forage providing roughly 40 percent of their annual feed and most permittees living in towns such as Boulder, Escalante, Kanab, Glendale, Orderville, Panguitch, and Tropic. Agriculture provides under 15 percent of employment and less than 5 percent of personal income, and both counties have seen population declines, while recreational users of Bryce Canyon and Glen Canyon, hunters, and downstream Colorado River water users are also affected.

alternatives_overview.text: Six alternatives are analyzed: (1) Continuation of Present Management at 109,708 AUMs of grazing preference; (2) Elimination of Livestock Grazing, allocating all forage to wildlife and wild horses; (3) Multiple Resource Enhancement, sharply reducing grazing to protect watersheds and riparian zones; (4) Adjustment to Grazing Capacity at 68,298 AUMs with limited new developments; (5) the BLM-preferred Rangeland Management Recommendation, growing from 68,298 to 91,444 AUMs through grazing systems, water and fence developments, and vegetation treatments; and (6) Livestock Optimization, reaching about 104,343 long-term AUMs through the most extensive vegetation treatments. The Kane and Garfield County Commissions recommended a hybrid of Alternatives 1 and 6.

environmental_impact.text: Inventories show soils, vegetation, and wildlife are mostly in poor to fair condition, with about 38 percent of suitable rangeland in poor forage condition, 12 percent of soils in severe or critical erosion, 44 percent of riparian miles in poor condition, and big-game habitat largely degraded with mule deer numbers down to about 5,539 from 17,044. Continuing present management would worsen forage, soils, and deer habitat, while eliminating grazing would substantially improve riparian areas, soils (211,366 acres of critical erosion improved), water quality, and wildlife but at high cost to ranchers. The preferred Alternative 5 would improve vegetation production, watershed condition, wildlife habitat, and rancher income over time, though vegetation treatments (burning, chaining, plowing, spraying) and new fences and water developments would cause short-term soil losses, sediment increases, and visual impacts in high-visibility areas, and salinity and sediment in the Colorado River basin remain key concerns.

public_response.text: Sixteen scoping meetings (April–November 1979) and public hearings in Kanab and Escalante (June 1980) drew testimony from federal and state agencies, county commissions, ranchers, and conservation groups, with a public comment period extended from 45 to 60 days. Ranchers, the Kane and Garfield County Commissions, and Utah's Governor strongly opposed proposed AUM reductions, citing prior cumulative cuts (up to 68–71 percent for some permittees), unfulfilled BLM commitments to seedings and water developments, drought-skewed inventory data, understated economic impacts, and rejection of grazing being treated as a privilege rather than a right. Wildlife and environmental groups (e.g., Utah Wilderness Association, Wild Horse Organized Assistance) argued the alternatives did not adequately protect riparian areas, wild horses, or wildlife forage, and EPA rated the EIS as ER-2, urging stronger emphasis on reducing saline runoff to the Colorado River.

value.name: Alternative 1 – Continuation of Present Management
value.description: Present grazing practices (season, level, and kind of management) would continue with a livestock forage allocation remaining at the current grazing preference of 109,708 AUMs. No adjustment to season, level, or kind of management would be made, and no forage allocation to wildlife or wild horses would be established.

value.name: Alternative 2 – Elimination of Livestock Grazing
value.description: All authorized livestock use on the entire 2,567,466 acres of public land in the K/E EIS area would be eliminated, with the 68,298 AUMs of available livestock forage reallocated to wildlife and wild horses. The purpose is to promote fast recovery of vegetation, water quality, wildlife, and wild horse habitat condition and to reduce soil erosion.

value.name: Alternative 3 – Multiple Resource Enhancement
value.description: Livestock forage allocation would be reduced to 35,224 AUMs (a 49-percent reduction from average active authorized use) with additional deductions to reduce soil erosion and enhance vegetation, wildlife habitat, water quality, floodplains, and riparian habitat. After two years, 9,948 AUMs in 34 allotments would be restored to livestock management for a total of 45,172 AUMs.

value.name: Alternative 4 – Adjustment to Grazing Capacity
value.description: Livestock grazing use would be initially limited to 68,298 AUMs (the available forage from the BLM Range Survey) with no new grazing management systems proposed, and proposed developments and vegetation treatments limited to the 21 existing Allotment Management Plans. The purpose is to manage resources within surveyed capacity at minimal cost.

value.name: Alternative 5 – Rangeland Management Recommendation (BLM Preferred)
value.description: The BLM preferred alternative would provide an initial livestock allocation of 68,298 AUMs with a proposed long-term allocation of 91,444 AUMs, implementing specific management systems on 129 allotments and developing all potentially suitable areas to optimize grazing animal distribution. Wild horses would be removed from the K/E EIS area and their 314 AUMs reallocated to bighorn sheep.

value.name: Alternative 6 – Livestock Optimization
value.description: Initially identical to Alternative 5 in management systems and forage allocations, this alternative would provide an additional 16,259 AUMs of livestock forage within 24 years through intensive vegetation treatment, bringing total long-term livestock forage allocation to 104,343 AUMs. Wild horses would be removed from the K/E EIS area.

themes.justification: The EIS centers on BLM's regulatory management of grazing allotments affecting degraded wildlife habitat (mule deer decline, riparian conditions) and watershed health, while the public_response field documents strong community resistance from ranchers and county commissions opposing AUM reductions they view as threatening their land-use rights and livelihoods.

location.note: Grazing management EIS covering 2,567,466 acres of public land in Washington, Kane, and Garfield Counties in Utah and Coconino County in Arizona, centered on the Kanab/Escalante area.

cooperating_agencies.name: USDA Agricultural Stabilization and Conservation Service
USDA Forest Service – Dixie National Forest
USDA Forest Service – Regional Office, Region 4
Department of the Army, Corps of Engineers
Utah State Historic Preservation Office, Department of Development Services
Utah Division of Parks and Recreation
Utah Division of State Lands – Forestry and Fire Control
